new_findings.topic: PPV value ladder / explicit content gating
new_findings.title: Gate explicit PPV behind $200+ in-session spend to preserve perceived value

variants.claim: Creators should never sell explicit content until a fan has spent at least $200 in that session, using Instagram-story-style content at $30–$40 as the early rungs of the ladder.
variants.what_they_do: The agency sells non-explicit, Instagram-story-style content priced at $30–$40 per piece first. Only after a fan has spent a minimum of $200 in a single session do chatters unlock access to explicit PPV. The threshold Griffin mentions for 'raw' content is $400 cumulative spend. This is enforced via chat scripts and objection-handling sequences, not left to chatter discretion.

sources.quote: we will sell Instagram story style of content for $30, $40, sell another thing that can go on Instagram for $30 $40 and eventually after they spend minimum 400 get into the actual raw stuff
our creators, they will not sell anything that is explicit if the person has not spent minimum $200 to get that. And that's not lifetime. That's in that one session.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is outsourced (§8) and Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or spend-gating rule (§7 GAP). This is a direct build-toward finding for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat SOPs, codify a session-spend gate: chatters may not offer explicit PPV until the fan has spent ≥$200 in that conversation. Define 2–3 non-explicit 'entry' content types priced $30–$40 that serve as the opening rungs. Document this as a hard rule in the chatter playbook, not a guideline.

new_findings.topic: Sales call framework — rapport, authority, pain points, CTA
new_findings.title: Three-pillar model sales call: rapport → authority/pain discovery → CTA

variants.claim: Model sales calls should follow a strict three-part structure — rapport first (3–5 min), then establish authority while uncovering pain points, then pitch — to maximize signing rate without seeming pushy.
variants.what_they_do: First 3–5 minutes: pure rapport (location, lifestyle, nothing business). Second pillar: either demonstrate authority (quote comparable creator results) or uncover pain points (time, chatting burden, posting), then position the agency as the solution to what she actually wants (travel, freedom) — not just more money. Third pillar: relaxed CTA framing around 'only making deals that work for both sides,' then ask for the close and handle objections. DM outreach tone is casual voice-message style, explicitly non-corporate.

sources.quote: The three pillars that I like to break it down into is rapport and establish a relationship. The second one... discover pain points. And the third is CTA.

if you did have that time, just like out of my own curiosity, what are you missing out on? Is it just feeling like too much of a hassle in general? Or are you really wanting to put more time into something else?

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented sales call script or framework (§6 GAP). This is a directly actionable framework to fill that gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write a Palm model sales call playbook using this three-pillar structure. Script the rapport opener, the authority one-liner template (insert Palm's real creator results), the pain-point discovery questions, and the CTA close. Train Josh (and future acquisition staff) on it. Also standardize the DM outreach tone to casual/voice-message style rather than formal pitch.

new_findings.topic: Model churn prevention — timeline setting and creator quality filter
new_findings.title: Set realistic timelines at signing and drop underperforming creators early to prevent churn

variants.claim: Most model churn comes from overpromises, lack of results, or poor communication — and is preventable by setting honest timelines at close and only signing creators who will match agency effort.
variants.what_they_do: At the end of every sales call, Griffin explicitly tells models that OF takes time and effort, makes only realistic revenue promises tied to honest assessments, and commits to open communication. If a creator is not producing quality content or effort after 3–4 weeks despite correct agency execution, she is dropped rather than nursed along. The filtering standard: only sign creators whose looks, consistency, and content quality can actually produce results.

sources.quote: There's overpromises, there's lack of results just in general, and then there's lack of communication. Those are the main reasons why I've seen models leave my own agency.
It's so much easier to spend 3 4 weeks signing one creator and looking for one specific creator than spending 3 4 weeks trying to make one creator work

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented retention strategy or churn-signal SOP (§9 GAP). The creator-quality filter is also undocumented in Palm's onboarding (§6). Palm onboarding collects creator info but has no explicit go/no-go quality criteria.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add two elements to Palm's process: (1) a closing script segment that sets an honest timeline with the creator before signing — include this in the onboarding call, not just the contract; (2) a 30-day creator performance review checkpoint (content volume, views, effort signals) with a documented drop criteria. Also define minimum creator quality standards (look tier, content consistency) as a pre-signing filter.

new_findings.topic: IG growth playbook — organic → promo → private/unprivate cycle
new_findings.title: Organic-to-promo-to-private/unprivate IG growth loop before pushing paid Reels ads

variants.claim: Growing a model's IG fast requires a specific sequenced loop: organic posts to 100 followers, model-to-model promo, privating the account to funnel promo traffic, unprivating, then organic Reels — only adding paid ads if organic Reels underperform.
variants.what_they_do: Start the page, post organically to ~100 followers. Reach out to other models for model-to-model promo or general promo. Private the account, run all promo traffic to it, then unprivate. Post Reels and evaluate performance. If Reels perform, continue organic. If not, layer in paid ad campaigns (boosted Reels). Competitor analysis is used to find which promo accounts established creators in the same niche are already using — DM those exact promo accounts directly.

sources.quote: start the page, post organically, and then once you hit around 100 followers, hit up some models, do model-to-model promo or just promotion in general, and then private the account, run all that traffic over to there, unprivate the account, post reels
you can see exactly what promo accounts they're using to get their results. And then you can just DM those promo accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's account warmup is mature for AI personas (§4) but thin/undocumented for real creators (§4 GAP). Palm also lacks a documented traffic/distribution strategy including promo (§5 GAP). This provides a concrete real-creator IG growth sequence Palm can formalize.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document this loop as Palm's real-creator IG warmup SOP: (1) post organically to 100 followers, (2) arrange model-to-model promo, (3) private account during promo burst, (4) unprivate and post Reels, (5) evaluate after 2 weeks — if underperforming, activate paid Reels boost. Also add competitor promo-account reverse-engineering (check competitors' tags to find their promo partners) as a standard promo sourcing step.

new_findings.topic: Reel ideation via niche-tuned algorithm phones
new_findings.title: Maintain dedicated niche-algorithm phones per creator cluster to source viral Reel ideas

variants.claim: Agencies should operate dedicated phones per niche group with Instagram algorithms trained exclusively on that niche, so the feed surfaces only relevant viral content for ideation.
variants.what_they_do: The agency assigns 1–2 phones per niche cluster (e.g., all curvy models share a phone). Those phones exclusively follow, like, and interact with accounts in that niche so the IG algorithm feeds only niche-relevant viral content. Managers scroll those feeds to pull Reel ideas organically rather than searching. This is paired with direct competitor account monitoring. Additionally, creators are shown the actual competitor content being referenced so they understand the creative direction and can self-direct, reducing the revision loop.

sources.quote: we'll have an algorithm that just gets fed curvy model niche videos. And so what that is is you just have to like, interact, follow all of the accounts that are like her.

make sure your models know what type of content they're making, what the message is supposed to be. Make them honestly look at the competitors that you're looking at so they know exactly what to produce

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) uses Apify scraping from curated inspo accounts — a more automated, data-driven version of this same principle. Palm is ahead here systematically. However, the niche-algorithm phone approach is a low-tech complement that could surface trends Apify misses (e.g., from accounts not yet in Palm's inspo source list). Palm also does not document sharing the full creative strategy/competitor context with creators to reduce revision cycles.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Palm's Apify pipeline already covers the core of this. Recommend two additions: (1) consider maintaining 1–2 niche-trained phones as a supplemental ideation source for niches where Palm's inspo account list is sparse; (2) when promoting a filming idea to a creator's board (Step 5 of Palm's pipeline), include 2–3 competitor reference videos so creators understand the full creative intent — this should reduce the revision loop in the editor workflow.

new_findings.topic: Hiring timing relative to growth stage
new_findings.title: Pursue vertical creator growth first; hire horizontally only after first creator is scaled

variants.claim: Agency owners should fully scale their first creator (vertical growth) before hiring team members, and should not split revenue with partners for tasks one person can handle alone.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin did not split money with other people until the agency was above $75K/month. His framework: first pursue 'vertical growth' — scale one creator as high as possible, get chatters in place. Only after hitting a stable horizontal plateau does he add roles like content manager or chat manager. He warns against early partnerships where two people split tasks that one person could do, bleeding revenue unnecessarily.

sources.quote: I only started splitting money with people when I was making above $75,000 a month.
once you have that vertical growth, you get her to XYZ number that you want, then look for horizontal... Once you're at the horizontal point is when you should start hiring.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has two founders (Josh + Evan) with distinct roles (acquisition vs. tech/ops) and an external chat team plus editors. The vertical-before-horizontal principle is relevant as Palm signs new creators — the caution against premature horizontal hiring before a creator is fully scaled is a useful internal decision framework Palm lacks in documented form (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a hiring decision framework for Palm: define what 'vertical scale achieved' looks like per creator (e.g., creator at revenue plateau, chat team in place, content pipeline stable) before headcount is added for that creator. Avoid assigning dedicated staff to a creator until she has demonstrated revenue stability. Use this as a gate in Palm's creator onboarding-to-operations handoff process.

new_findings.topic: Creator persona / perceived value through non-explicit social presence
new_findings.title: Post only gym pics and selfies on IG — zero explicit — to maximize PPV perceived value

variants.claim: Keeping a creator's social profile deliberately casual and non-explicit (gym photos, selfies) makes explicit OF content feel rare and premium, enabling higher PPV prices.
variants.what_they_do: The agency posts only non-explicit, personal-feeling content on Instagram — gym pictures, selfies, nothing polished or professional. The goal is a 'Snapchat story' intimacy feel rather than a branded creator aesthetic. This scarcity and informality is what allows explicit PPV to command high prices on OF.

sources.quote: on the profile of our creator, man, we will post gym pictures, selfies, nothing explicit at all. Literally almost like she's just doing some Snapchat stories. And that allows us to sell her explicit content for way more perceived value

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is strong on ideation and production but does not document a content-tone or explicitness-ceiling policy for social posts. This is a positioning/strategy layer missing from Palm's social content SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a content-tone policy to Palm's creator social SOP: define a maximum explicitness ceiling for IG/TikTok posts (e.g., no explicit content, limit professionally styled shoots) and frame this explicitly as a PPV value-preservation strategy. Communicate this rationale to creators so they don't push for more explicit social posts.

new_evidence.title: Structure chat team as manager + 3 shift chatters + 1 reliever per model; pay hourly + tiered commission

variants.claim: A properly structured chat team—chat manager, three 8-hour-shift chatters, and one reliever—with a calibrated hourly/commission mix is required for 24/7 monetization at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Team per model: 1 chat manager ($300–400/week), 3 chatters on 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage, 1 reliever covering days off. Manager trains new chatters, handles whales personally, reviews performance. Chatters: $2–5/hour + 2–8% commission. On large pages (30K+/month), shift to high hourly / zero-to-1% commission because commission payouts exceed hourly cost. European chatters (Serbia, Germany, France) preferred over Philippines for quality; Philippines at $4/hour with no commission if budget-constrained. Hire from large pools: Upwork, OnlineJobs.ph, OFM Telegram groups. Rule: get 500 applicants not 50; hire slow, fire fast. Monitor chatters live during first two shifts; require end-of-shift screenshot reports thereafter. Manage entire team in a structured Discord hub (clock-in/out, break channels, end-of-shift reports, chatter monitoring).

variants.claim: Agency owners should chat themselves until $5K/month, then hire 1–2 chatters, scaling to a full team (chat manager + 4 chatters per model) only at $10K+; pay hourly and commission in inverse proportion.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin chats personally until the agency hits $5K/month. At $5K he brings in 1–2 chatters. Full chat team structure (1 chat manager + 4 chatters) targets $10K+ monthly. Chatter pay: industry standard $2–$5/hr and 1–5% commission. His own agency runs $2/hr + 4% commission. Key mechanic: if commission is low, raise hourly; if hourly is low, raise commission — mirror the two to maintain incentive balance. He caps commission at 5% for higher-revenue accounts.

sources.quote: chatters plus one reliever per model... each chatter works eight hour shifts... three times eight, you have full 24-hour coverage
pay for this is going to be $2 to $5 an hour... commission is going to be 2 to 8% of sales that they generate
where to find them? Upwork, online jobs.ph, OFM specific Telegram groups
the benchmark you need to make money is around 5K and then once you hit that 5K you have allocated some money to put into VAS one to two chatters
the industry standard, what we do inside of our agency is $2 an hour at 4% commission. That kind of just creates a balance of both worlds.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8); no internal team structure, hiring criteria, compensation bands, or management tooling exists. This is the most detailed build-toward blueprint available for Palm's ~1yr in-house chat team roadmap. Palm has Telegram-based coordination infrastructure (§5) that could partially serve the Discord hub role.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this team structure as the staffing template for the in-house chat team build. Document comp bands (hourly + commission tiers by page revenue) in the pricing/commission SOP (§7 gap). Evaluate whether Palm's existing Telegram infrastructure can replace Discord for team management or whether a separate Discord hub is warranted. Begin sourcing from Upwork and OFM Telegram groups with a target pool of 100+ applicants for the first in-house chatter hire. Build the training document suite (model personality sheet, welcome SOP, fan-profile template) before the first hire.

_new_sources.quote: the benchmark you need to make money is around 5K and then once you hit that 5K you have allocated some money to put into VAS one to two chatters
the industry standard, what we do inside of our agency is $2 an hour at 4% commission. That kind of just creates a balance of both worlds.
